dialogue.utterance: Unit 4, respond for an allergic reaction with breathing difficulty at an apartment complex in [CITY].
Hello ma'am, my name is Jason and I'm an EMT. We're here to help you breathe easier. Can you tell me what's going on right now?
My wife's name is Carla and she woke up 30 minutes ago with facial droop and trouble speaking or swallowing.
I see, so the main issue seems to be trouble speaking, swallowing, and facial droop, is that right?
Yes, she can't move the left side of her face much, and her left foot is pointing downward.
Ms. Carla, can you hear me? What's your name?
She knows her name, but she smiled and gave a thumbs up when I told her mine.
Carla, can you follow my eyes with yours? They look clear and respond well.
Carla, today is the 23rd of November. Can you tell me what the date is?
She pointed to her calendar when I asked her and gave me a thumbs up when I mentioned today's date.
Carla, can you press your fingers into my hand? She has some spasms and is unable to move her left foot at all.
Carla, we're going to take a quick look at your airway. You're drooling on the left side.
She's drooling on the left side of her mouth, but she can manage her own secretions for now.
You seem to be breathing consistently, but we're going to check your rate for clarity.
She has regular breaths at 20 per minute. Oxygen saturation is 97% on room air.
We'll take your heart rate now. You seem a bit sweaty, yes?
Pulse is 100, strong and regular. Blood pressure is 156/106. Glucose is 126.
Carla, do you remember when your symptoms started?
She's had general weakness and fatigue all day, and about 30 minutes ago, she woke up like this-bizarre spasms and facial droop.
So she had weakness and fatigue today, and when she woke up, she showed spasms, trouble speaking, swallowing, and facial droop.
What happened right before the symptoms started today? Did anything trigger the spasms?
She was resting and didn't feel right. Then when she woke up, her face was off and her feet were turned left. She became very anxious.
Carla, how would you describe the spasms? Do they feel like sharp or jerking movements?
When these spasms start, is there anything you do that makes them worse?
On a scale of 0 to 10, how severe is the discomfort from the spasms and all the symptoms?
She didn't rate it on the scale, but she looked very uncomfortable with the spasms and drooling and was visibly scared.
Approximately how long have the spasms and facial droop been ongoing?
The spasms and facial droop started about 30 minutes ago, right when she got up from resting.
Can you see her face and eyes? She has left-sided facial droop and equal pupils reactive to light.
She can move her fingers but not her feet. Her feet are pointed medially and she can't dorsiflex them.
No neck rigidity or visual abnormalities; she's following commands with gestures.
She's diaphoretic but has no visible rashes or redness. Her skin appears a bit pale in some areas.
Pulse is 82 now, showing signs of improvement.
Respirations are still 20, even and unlabored.
Blood pressure is up to 179/117 now.
Oxygen saturation is at 94%-we're keeping track every few minutes.
Initial glucose was 126, but it became unreadable in subsequent testing.
Let's start an IV in case we need to give medication to help ease the spasms.
IV was started in her left wrist on the second try, and it's patent now with a flush and aspiration.
Let's set up a cardiac monitor for continuous observation.
We're going to give you 50 mg Benadryl intravenously now. That should help with the spasms and drooling.
Based on her symptoms and vitals, we're following protocol 3-3 for allergic reaction and dystonia.
Pulse is 89 and tachycardic again.
Blood pressure is now 146/106 with oxygen saturation at 99%.
Glucose remains unreadable.
We've started a 4-lead ECG showing sinus rhythm for now.
We need to take you to a hospital. Her initial choice was MCV, but they're not accepting non-trauma patients. She's okay with [COMPANY_NAME].
We'll now secure her to the stretcher. Let's gently move her into the ambulance.
She was able to get from the stretcher to the ED bed herself, with her improvement showing.
Ms. Carla is a 50-year-old with allergic reaction, dystonic symptoms including facial droop, difficulty speaking, and abnormal foot positioning.
She received 50 mg Benadryl IV and showed marked improvement in spasms, speech, and facial function post-treatment.
She has an allergy to Penicillin and Imitrex. Her medical history includes asthma, hypothyroidism, and depression.
We've been monitoring her closely and her condition has improved significantly during transport.
Vital signs improved with BP at 143/92, pulse 77, and O2 at 97%. She's now on a four-lead ECG showing sinus rhythm.
She has Medicaid but didn't have her card today. We did not carry any personal belongings for her.
Care is being transferred to the ED physician and RN. Crew is clear to return to service.
Her facial droop and verbal deficits have resolved post-treatment, so stroke is no longer a concern.
We chose [COMPANY_NAME] as they are accepting non-trauma patients and offer neurological support.
She was very appreciative during transport and remained stable with continued improvement.
She is now conversing and standing on her own with full return of function. The skin pallor and drooling have subsided.
She continues to be asymptomatic from her initial painful and neurological symptoms.
No changes in medical plan or interventions during transport. Treatment was successful and matched in-field diagnosis.
[COMPANY_NAME] is equipped to handle her ongoing care due to their emergency and neurological capabilities.
Pains initially felt and unresolved, but post-Benadryl administration, she reports no discomfort, aligning with zero pain score per EPCR.
Admission will include IV maintenance and follow-up with EMS crew as per protocol.
Final evaluation consistent with full recovery, showing no residual signs of weakness or drooling.
Thank you for your cooperation and helpful presence throughout this process, and we're glad to assist you, Mr. and Mrs. Carla.
